context_pack.exact_source_excerpt: \begin{cor} Let $S$ be a closed orientable surface with genus at least 2. Let $\alpha$ be a multicurve on $S$ and let $E_\alpha$ be the full preimage in $\operatorname{MCG}(S;z)$ of the stabilizer of $\alpha$ in $\operatorname{MCG}(S)$. \begin{enumerate} \item $E_\alpha$ has quadratic Dehn function. \item $E_\alpha$ has finitely many conjugacy classes of finite order subgroups. \item $E_\alpha$ is semi-hyperbolic and hence has solvable conjugacy problem. \end{enumerate} \end{cor}

context_pack.exact_source_excerpt: \begin{thm}\label{intro_thm:quasi-tree} The graph $X_\alpha$ is uniformly quasi-isometric to a tree and the group $E_\alpha$ has the following properties. \begin{itemize} \item $E_\alpha$ acts acylindrically on $X_\alpha$ and this action is largest in the sense of \emph{\cite{ABO_largest_action}}. \item A subgroup $H <E_\alpha$ is stable if and only if the orbit map of $H$ in $X_\alpha$ is a quasi-isometric embedding. In particular, every stable subgroup of $E_\alpha$ is virtually free. %In particular, every convex cocompact subgroup of $\operatorname{MCG}(S;z)$ that is contained in $E_\alpha$ is virtually free. \item The Morse boundary of $E_\alpha$ is an $\omega$-Cantor space. \end{itemize} \end{thm}
definitions: Let $S$ be a closed orientable surface with genus at least 2.
Let $\alpha$ be a multicurve on $S$ and let $E_\alpha$ be the full preimage in $\operatorname{MCG}(S;z)$ of the stabilizer of $\alpha$ in $\operatorname{MCG}(S)$.
let $S^z$ denote the surface obtained from $S$ by adding $z \in S$ as a marked point, and let $\Pi \colon S^z \to S$ be the map given by forgetting that $z$ is a marked point.
local_assumptions: if and only if the orbit map of $H$ in $X_\alpha$ is a quasi-isometric embedding.
nearby_claims: Theorem \ref{intro_thm:E_alpha_is_HHG} uses the recent \emph{combinatorial hierarchical hyperbolicity} machinery of Behrstock, Hagen, Martin, and Sisto \cite{BHMS_cHHS}.

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{lem:checking_coarsely_lipschitz}
	Let $X$ be a connected graph and $f \colon X^0 \to Y$ be a coarse map.  If there exist $\lambda\geq 1$ so that $\operatorname{diam}_Y(f(x)) \leq \lambda$ for each $x$ and  $d_Y(f(x_1),f(x_2)) \leq \lambda$ for every $x_1,x_2 \in X^0$ that are joined by an edge, then the map $f \colon X \to Y$ induced by the map on the vertices is $(3\lambda,0)$-coarsely Lipschitz coarse map.
semantic_equivalence_artifact.normalized_natural_language_theorem: Quantifiers: for each; for every Assumptions: X is a connected graph; f : X^0 → Y is a coarse map; λ ≥ 1; diam_Y(f(x)) ≤ λ for each x; d_Y(f(x_1), f(x_2)) ≤ λ for every x_1, x_2 ∈ X^0 joined by an edge Conclusion: The map f : X → Y induced by the map on the vertices is (3λ, 0)-coarsely Lipschitz coarse map.

context_pack.exact_source_excerpt: \begin{lem}\label{lem:coarsel_lipschitz} Let $H$ and $Y$ be connected graphs equipped with the path metric. Suppose that $H$ is a subgraph of $Y$. If there exists a coarse map $\psi \colon Y \to H$ so that \begin{itemize} \item $\psi$ is the identity on $H^0$; \item $\psi$ is $(\lambda,\epsilon)$-coarsely Lipschitz; \end{itemize} then, the inclusion of $H$ into $Y$ is a $(\lambda,\epsilon+2\lambda)$-quasi-isometric embedding. \end{lem}

Let $x,x' \in X$ and $x_0,x_1,\dots,x_n$ be the vertices on a geodesic in $X$ connecting $x$ to $x'$.
Let $H$ and $Y$ be connected graphs equipped with the path metric.
Let $h_1,h_2 \in H^0$.

if \[d_Y(f(x_1),f(x_2)) \leq \lambda d_X(x_1,x_2) + \epsilon\] for each $x_1,x_2 \in X$.

u \in \mathcal{M}(S)$ if $d_U(\mu,
u)\leq r$ for each non-pants subsurface $U \subseteq S$, then $d_{\mathcal{M}(S)}(\mu,
u) \leq f(r)$. \item \label{item:cleaning} There is $D>0$ depending only on $S$ so that for each (not necessarily clean) marking $\mu$, there exists a set of clean markings $\operatorname{cl}(\mu)$ satisfying:  egin{itemize} \item $\operatorname{base}(\mu') = \operatorname{base}(\mu)$ for each $\mu' \in \operatorname{cl}(\mu)$; \item if $\mu' \in \operatorname{cl}(\mu)$, then for each $(c_i,t_i)\in \mu$ and $(c_i,t_i') \in \mu'$, we have $d_{U_i}(t_i,t_i') \leq 3$ where $U_i$ is the annulus with core curve $c_i$; \item $\operatorname{cl}(\mu)$ has diameter at most $D$ in $\mathcal{M}(S)$. \end{itemize} \end{enumerate} \end{thm}

translation_statement_schema.claim:
The marking graph $\mathcal{M}(S)$ is a proper connected graph with the following properties: 1) The action of $\operatorname{MCG}(S)$ on $S$ induces a metrically proper and cocompact action of $\operatorname{MCG}(S)$ on $\mathcal{M}(S)$ by isometries. 2) There is a function $f \colon \[0,\infty) 	o \[0,\infty)$ depending only on $S$ so that for any two clean markings $\mu,
u \in \mathcal{M}(S)$ if $d_U(\mu,
u)\leq r$ for each non-pants subsurface $U \subseteq S$, then $d_{\mathcal{M}(S)}(\mu,
u) \leq f(r)$. 3) There is $D>0$ depending only on $S$ so that for each (not necessarily clean) marking $\mu$, there exists a set of clean markings $\operatorname{cl}(\mu)$ satisfying: $\operatorname{base}(\mu') = \operatorname{base}(\mu)$ for each $\mu' \in \operatorname{cl}(\mu)$; if $\mu' \in \operatorname{cl}(\mu)$, then for each $(c_i,t_i)\in \mu$ and $(c_i,t_i') \in \mu'$, we have $d_{U_i}(t_i,t_i') \leq 3$ where $U_i$ is the annulus with core curve $c_i$; $\operatorname{cl}(\mu)$ has diameter at most $D$ in $\mathcal{M}(S)$.
translation_statement_schema.theorem_intent: To establish properties of the marking graph $\mathcal{M}(S)$, including its proper connectedness, the action of the mapping class group, a distance formula, and a cleaning property for markings.

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{ex:saturation}
	If $\Delta$ is a simplex of $\mathcal{C}(S)$, then $\Delta$ defines a subsurface $U_\Delta$ that is the disjoint union of all the non-pants components of $S - \Delta$.  The link of $\Delta$ in $\mathcal{C}(S)$ is then spanned by all curves on $U_\Delta$, that is, $\operatorname{lk}(\Delta) = \mathcal{C}(U_\Delta)$. Two simplices $\Delta$ and $\Delta'$  will therefore have equal links if and only if $U_\Delta = U_{\Delta'}$. Further, the saturation of $\Delta$ will be the set of curves of on $S - U_\Delta$ plus the curves in $\partial U_\Delta$, that is, $\operatorname{Sat}(\Delta) = \mathcal{C}^0(S - U_\Delta) \cup \partial U_\Delta$. This is because every non-peripheral curve on $S-U_\Delta$ is part of a pants decomposition of $S-U_\Delta$, and the join of this pants decomposition with $\partial U_\Delta$ produces a simplex $\Delta'$ with $U_{\Delta'} = U_\Delta$.

entries.lean_statement:
theorem example_20
  {S : Type*} [MetricSpace S] [Fintype S]
  (H : Type*) [MetricSpace H]
  (U : Type*) [MetricSpace U]
  (delta delta' : Type*) [MetricSpace delta] [MetricSpace delta']
  (Y : Type*) [MetricSpace Y]
  (pi : Y → H)
  (U_delta U_delta' : Subtype U)
  (lk : Type*) [MetricSpace lk]
  (overlap : U_delta → U_delta' → Prop)
  (largest_subsurface : U_delta → U_delta' → Subtype U)
  (simplex : Type*) [MetricSpace simplex]
  (Γ : simplex)
  (pants_decomposition : simplex → simplex → Prop)
  (boundary_curves : Subtype U → Set simplex)
  (filling : Set simplex → Set simplex → Prop)
  (complexity_one : Set simplex → Prop)
  (h_quasi : ∀ x y, overlap x y → ∃ (C : ℝ), ∀ a b, pi a = x → pi b = y → dist a b ≤ C * (dist x y + 1))
  (h_lipschitz : ∀ x y, pi x = pi y → dist x y ≤ dist (pi x) (pi y))
  (h_link : ∀ delta1 delta2, overlap delta1 delta2 → ∃ (C : ℝ), dist delta1 delta2 ≤ C)
  (h_largest : ∀ delta1 delta2, overlap delta1 delta2 → largest_subsurface delta1 delta2 ⊆ U_delta)
  (h_pants : ∀ delta1 delta2, overlap delta1 delta2 → pants_decomposition delta1 delta2)
  (h_boundary : ∀ delta1 delta2, overlap delta1 delta2 → boundary_curves (largest_subsurface delta1 delta2) ⊆ Set.univ)
  (h_filling : ∀ x y, overlap x y → filling {x} {y})
  (h_complexity : ∀ x y, filling {x} {y} → complexity_one {x})
  : ∃ (C : ℝ), ∀ x y, pi x = x → pi y = y → dist x y ≤ C * (dist (pi x) (pi y) + 1)

assumptions.label: h_largest
assumptions.lean_expr: (h_largest : ∀ delta1 delta2, overlap delta1 delta2 → largest_subsurface delta1 delta2 ⊆ U_delta)

assumptions.label: h_pants
assumptions.lean_expr: (h_pants : ∀ delta1 delta2, overlap delta1 delta2 → pants_decomposition delta1 delta2)

assumptions.label: h_filling
assumptions.lean_expr: (h_filling : ∀ x y, overlap x y → filling {x} {y})

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
For $(\mathcal{C}(S),\mathcal{P}(S))$, the spaces $H(\Delta)$ are hyperbolic because they are quasi-isometric to $\mathcal{C}(U_\Delta)$. This is because distances in $\mathcal{C}(U_\Delta)$ are bounded above by the intersection number of curves. Because the space $Y_\Delta$ is spanned by curves that intersect the subsurface $U_\Delta$, subsurface projection gives a coarsely Lipschitz map $\pi_{U_\Delta} \colon Y_\Delta \to H(\Delta)$. This makes $H(\Delta)$ quasi-isometrically embedded in $Y_\Delta$ by Lemma \ref{lem:coarsel_lipschitz}.
	
	For the combinatorial conditions, observe that $\operatorname{lk}(\Delta) \subseteq \operatorname{lk}(\Delta')$ if and only if $U_\Delta \subseteq  U_{\Delta'}$. Thus, the finite complexity of $S$ gives a uniform bound on chains of properly nested links of simplices. For Item \ref{CHHS:comb_nesting_condition}, it suffices to verify the condition for simplices $\Delta_1$ and $\Delta_2$ where $U_{\Delta_1}$ and $U_{\Delta_2}$ overlap.  Let $Q$ be the largest subsurface contained in both $U_{\Delta_1}$ and $U_{\Delta_2}$, then let $\Gamma$ be a simplex so that $\Delta_2 \bowtie \Gamma$ is a pants decomposition of   $S -Q$, The desired simplex $\Omega$ will be $\Gamma$ joined with the set of boundary curves of $Q$  that are not curves of $\Delta_2$. For Item \ref{CHHS:C=C_0_condition},  if $x$ and $y$ are two curves joined by a $\mathcal{P}(S)$-edge but not a $\mathcal{C}(S)$-edge, then $x$ and $y$ fill a complexity 1 subsurface $V$. If $x$ and $y$ are in the link of $\Delta$, then $V\subseteq U_\Delta$. Hence, we can find $\Lambda_x$ and $\Lambda_y$ by taking the join of $\Delta$ with $\partial U_\Delta$ and pants decompositions of $U_\Delta$ that contain $x$ and $y$ respectively.

context_pack.original_latex_theorem:
For $(\mathcal{C}(S),\mathcal{P}(S))$, the spaces $H(\Delta)$ are hyperbolic because they are quasi-isometric to $\mathcal{C}(U_\Delta)$. This is because distances in $\mathcal{C}(U_\Delta)$ are bounded above by the intersection number of curves. Because the space $Y_\Delta$ is spanned by curves that intersect the subsurface $U_\Delta$, subsurface projection gives a coarsely Lipschitz map $\pi_{U_\Delta} \colon Y_\Delta \to H(\Delta)$. This makes $H(\Delta)$ quasi-isometrically embedded in $Y_\Delta$ by Lemma \ref{lem:coarsel_lipschitz}.
	
	For the combinatorial conditions, observe that $\operatorname{lk}(\Delta) \subseteq \operatorname{lk}(\Delta')$ if and only if $U_\Delta \subseteq  U_{\Delta'}$. Thus, the finite complexity of $S$ gives a uniform bound on chains of properly nested links of simplices. For Item \ref{CHHS:comb_nesting_condition}, it suffices to verify the condition for simplices $\Delta_1$ and $\Delta_2$ where $U_{\Delta_1}$ and $U_{\Delta_2}$ overlap.  Let $Q$ be the largest subsurface contained in both $U_{\Delta_1}$ and $U_{\Delta_2}$, then let $\Gamma$ be a simplex so that $\Delta_2 \bowtie \Gamma$ is a pants decomposition of   $S -Q$, The desired simplex $\Omega$ will be $\Gamma$ joined with the set of boundary curves of $Q$  that are not curves of $\Delta_2$. For Item \ref{CHHS:C=C_0_condition},  if $x$ and $y$ are two curves joined by a $\mathcal{P}(S)$-edge but not a $\mathcal{C}(S)$-edge, then $x$ and $y$ fill a complexity 1 subsurface $V$. If $x$ and $y$ are in the link of $\Delta$, then $V\subseteq U_\Delta$. Hence, we can find $\Lambda_x$ and $\Lambda_y$ by taking the join of $\Delta$ with $\partial U_\Delta$ and pants decompositions of $U_\Delta$ that contain $x$ and $y$ respectively.
context_pack.exact_source_excerpt: \begin{example} For $(\mathcal{C}(S),\mathcal{P}(S))$, the spaces $H(\Delta)$ are hyperbolic because they are quasi-isometric to $\mathcal{C}(U_\Delta)$. This is because distances in $\mathcal{C}(U_\Delta)$ are bounded above by the intersection number of curves. Because the space $Y_\Delta$ is spanned by curves that intersect the subsurface $U_\Delta$, subsurface projection gives a coarsely Lipschitz map $\pi_{U_\Delta} \colon Y_\Delta \to H(\Delta)$. This makes $H(\Delta)$ quasi-isometrically embedded in $Y_\Delta$ by Lemma \ref{lem:coarsel_lipschitz}. For the combinatorial conditions, observe that $\operatorname{lk}(\Delta) \subseteq \operatorname{lk}(\Delta')$ if and only if $U_\Delta \subseteq U_{\Delta'}$. Thus, the finite complexity of $S$ gives a uniform bound on chains of properly nested links of simplices. For Item \ref{CHHS:comb_nesting_condition}, it suffices to verify the condition for simplices $\Delta_1$ and $\Delta_2$ where $U_{\Delta_1}$ and $U_{\Delta_2}$ overlap. Let $Q$ be the largest subsurface contained in both $U_{\Delta_1}$ and $U_{\Delta_2}$, then let $\Gamma$ be a simplex so that $\Delta_2 \bowtie \Gamma$ is a pants decomposition of $S -Q$, The desired simplex $\Omega$ will be $\Gamma$ joined with the set of boundary curves of $Q$ that are not curves of $\Delta_2$. For Item \ref{CHHS:C=C_0_condition}, if $x$ and $y$ are two curves joined by a $\mathcal{P}(S)$-edge but not a $\mathcal{C}(S)$-edge, then $x$ and $y$ fill a complexity 1 subsurface $V$. If $x$ and $y$ are in the link of $\Delta$, then $V\subseteq U_\Delta$. Hence, we can find $\Lambda_x$ and $\Lambda_y$ by taking the join of $\Delta$ with $\partial U_\Delta$ and pants decompositions of $U_\Delta$ that contain $x$ and $y$ respectively. \end{example}

context_pack.exact_source_excerpt: \begin{thm}[{\cite[Theorem 1.18, Remark 1.19]{BHMS_cHHS}}]\label{thm:CHHS_are_HHS} If $(X,W)$ is a $\delta$-combinatorial HHS, then $W$ is connected and a hierarchically hyperbolic space. Further, a finitely generated group $G$ will be a hierarchically hyperbolic group if \begin{enumerate} \item $G$ acts on $X$ by simplicial automorphisms with finitely many orbits of links of simplices; \item the action of $G$ on maximal simplices of $X$ induces a metrically proper and cobounded action of $G$ on $W$ by isometries. \end{enumerate} \end{thm}

	Let $X$ be a graph. Suppose that there exists a constant $R$ with the following property: for each pair of vertices $w, w' \in X$ there exists an edge path $\eta(w,w')$  from $w$ to $w'$ so that for any vertex $v$ of $\eta(w,w')$, any path from $w$ to $w'$ in $X$ intersects $\operatorname{ball}_R(v)$. Then $X$ is quasi-isometric to a tree, with quasi-isometry constants depending on $R$ only.

theorem lem_top_CHHS_dictionary (X_alpha : Type*) (S^z : Type*) (mu mu' : X_alpha)
    (NonMaximal : X_alpha → Prop) (lk : X_alpha → Set (X_alpha^0))
    (U_mu : Set S^z) (Sat : X_alpha → Set (X_alpha^0))
    (Disconnected : Set S^z → Prop) (IsJoin : Set (X_alpha^0) → Prop)
    (AnnularComponent : Set S^z → Type*) (CoreCurve : AnnularComponent (Set S^z) → X_alpha^0)
    (hmu : NonMaximal mu) (hmu' : NonMaximal mu') :
    (lk mu).Spans {x | x ∈ X_alpha^0 ∧ x ⊆ U_mu} ∧ (Disconnected U_mu → IsJoin (lk mu)) ∧
    (lk mu ⊆ lk mu' ↔ U_mu ⊆ U_mu') ∧
    (lk mu = lk mu' ↔ U_mu = U_mu') ∧
    Sat mu = {x | x ∈ X_alpha^0 ∧ x ⊆ (S^z \ U_mu)} ∪ {x | x ∈ d_dtU_mu ∧ ¬∃ A, AnnularComponent U_mu A ∧ x = CoreCurve A}

In particular, $\operatorname{lk}(\mu)$ is a join whenever $U_\mu$ is disconnected. \item \label{dictionary:nesting} $\operatorname{lk}(\mu) \subseteq \operatorname{lk}(\mu')$ if and only if $U_\mu \subseteq U_{\mu'}$. \item \label{dictionary:equivalenet} $\operatorname{lk}(\mu) = \operatorname{lk}(\mu')$ if and only if $U_\mu = U_{\mu'}$. \item

context_pack.exact_source_excerpt: \begin{lem}\label{lem:B-links} For any non-maximal simplex $\mu$ of $B(X_\alpha)$, the link of $\mu$ in $B(X_\alpha)$ is the subgraph spanned by the union of the following vertices: \begin{enumerate} \item The vertices of $B(c)$ for each $c \in \operatorname{unmark}(\mu)$. \item The set of vertices $\{x \in B^0(X_\alpha) : \operatorname{supp}(x) \in \operatorname{lk}_{X}(\operatorname{supp}(\mu))\}$. %\item The vertices of $X_\alpha$ that are contained in $\operatorname{lk}_{X}(\operatorname{supp}(\mu))$. %\item The vertices of $B(c)$ for each $c$ in $\operatorname{lk}_{X}(\operatorname{supp}(\mu))$. \item The vertices of $\operatorname{supp}(\mu) - \operatorname{base}(\mu)$. \end{enumerate} Moreover, if $\operatorname{supp}(\mu) \neq \operatorname{base}(\mu)$, then $\operatorname{lk}_{B}(\mu)$ is either a single vertex (which will be $\operatorname{supp}(\mu) - \operatorname{base}(\mu)$) or is a join of some graph with $\operatorname{supp}(\mu) -\operatorname{base}(\mu)$. \end{lem}
definitions: Let $x,y$ be vertices of $B(X_\alpha)$ that are joined by an edge.

context_pack.exact_source_excerpt: \begin{lem}\label{lem:B-nesting} Suppose $\mu$ and $\mu'$ are non-maximal simplices of $B(X_\alpha)$ with $\operatorname{supp}(\mu) = \operatorname{base}(\mu)$ and $\operatorname{supp}(\mu') = \operatorname{base}(\mu')$. We have $\operatorname{lk}_{B}(\mu) \subseteq \operatorname{lk}_{B}(\mu')$ if and only if \begin{itemize} \item $U_{\operatorname{supp}(\mu)} \subseteq U_{\operatorname{supp}(\mu')}$; and \item for each $c \in \operatorname{unmark}(\mu)$, either $c \in \operatorname{unmark}(\mu')$ or $c \subseteq U_{\operatorname{supp}(\mu')}$. \end{itemize} \end{lem}

    {alpha : Type*} [MeasurableSpace alpha]

context_pack.section: Reducing the geometric conditions to the non-annular case
context_pack.exact_source_excerpt: \begin{prop}\label{prop:geometric_conditions_annular_case} For each non-maximal simplex $\mu$ of $B(X_\alpha)$, the space $BH(\mu)$ is hyperbolic and the inclusion of $BH(\mu)$ into $B(Y_\mu)$ is a uniform quasi-isometric embedding. Moreover, $BH(\mu)$ falls into one of three cases: \begin{itemize} \item $BH(\mu)$ has bounded diameter because $\operatorname{lk}_{B}(\mu)$ is a join or a single vertex. \item $\operatorname{base}(\mu) =\operatorname{supp}(\mu)$, $\operatorname{base}(\mu)$ is not a pants decomposition of $S^z$, and $\mu$ contains no unmarked base curves. In this case, the inclusion of $H(\operatorname{base}(\mu))$ into $BH(\mu)$ is a uniform quasi-isometry. \item $\operatorname{base}(\mu)$ is a pants decomposition of $S^z$ and $\mu$ contains exactly one unmarked base curve $c$. In this case, $BH(\mu)$ is equal to $\mathcal{C}(A_c)$. \end{itemize} \end{prop}
nearby_claims: Lemma \ref{lem:forget_marking} showed this map is $(1,0)$-coarsely Lipschitz, making the inclusion of $X_\alpha$ into $B(X_\alpha)$ an isometric embedding.

[{\cite[Theorem 3.7]{ABD}}]\label{thm:ABD}
	Let $(G,\mathfrak{S})$ be a hierarchically hyperbolic group. There exists a hierarchically hyperbolic groups structure $\mathfrak{T}$ for $G$ with unbounded products. Moreover, if for  every non-$\sqsubseteq$-maximal $U \in \mathfrak{S}$ with  $\operatorname{diam}(\mathcal{H}(U)) = \infty$ there is $V \in \mathfrak{S}$ so that $V \perp U$ and $\operatorname{diam}(\mathcal{H}(V)) = \infty$, then the $\sqsubseteq$-maximal hyperbolic space of $\mathfrak{T}$ can be taken to be the $\sqsubseteq$-maximal hyperbolic space of $\mathfrak{S}$.

context_pack.exact_source_excerpt: \begin{thm}[\cite{ABD}]\label{thm:largest_action} Let $(G,\mathfrak{T})$ be a hierarchically hyperbolic group with unbounded products and let $T$ be the $\sqsubseteq$-maximal element of $\mathfrak{T}$. \begin{itemize} \item The action of $G$ on $\mathcal{H}(T)$ is the largest cobounded acylindrical action of $G$ on a hyperbolic space. \item A subgroup $H<G$ is stable if and only if the orbit map of $H$ into $\mathcal{H}(T)$ is a quasi-isometric embedding. \end{itemize} \end{thm}

if $\gamma$ is $N$-Morse, then there exists $\lambda\geq 1$ depending on $N$, $k$, and $c$, so that $\pi \circ \gamma$ is a $(\lambda,\lambda)$-quasi-geodesic of $X$.
if $\pi \circ \gamma$ is an $(\lambda,\lambda)$-quasi-geodesic, then $\gamma$ is $N$-Morse for some $N$ determined by $k$, $c$, and $\lambda$.
If the space $X$ is a quasi-tree, then we say $G$ is \emph{Morse detectable in a quasi-tree}.

In particular, every stable subgroup of $E_\alpha$ is virtually free. \item\label{item:Morse} The Morse boundary of $E_\alpha$ is an $\omega$-Cantor set. \end{enumerate} \end{cor}

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem: Let $H$ be the handlebody group of a genus $2$ handlebody. Che sser proved $H$ has a hierarchically hyperbolic group structure with unbounded products \cite{Chesser_genus_2_handlebody}. Further, Chesser shows that the $\sqsubseteq$-maximal space in this structure is a quasi-tree because it is the contact graph of a CAT(0) cube complex (they also prove this space is quasi-isometric to the disk graph of the genus 2 handlebody). Thus, $\partial_M H$ is an $\omega$-Cantor space. In the case of genus 3 or larger, the handlebody group is known to not be hierarchically hyperbolic \cite{Hamenstadt_Hensel_Handlebody} although the question of whether it is or is not Morse detectable is open.

context_pack.exact_source_excerpt: \begin{exappend} Let $H$ be the handlebody group of a genus $2$ handlebody. Che sser proved $H$ has a hierarchically hyperbolic group structure with unbounded products \cite{Chesser_genus_2_handlebody}. Further, Chesser shows that the $\sqsubseteq$-maximal space in this structure is a quasi-tree because it is the contact graph of a CAT(0) cube complex (they also prove this space is quasi-isometric to the disk graph of the genus 2 handlebody). Thus, $\partial_M H$ is an $\omega$-Cantor space. In the case of genus 3 or larger, the handlebody group is known to not be hierarchically hyperbolic \cite{Hamenstadt_Hensel_Handlebody} although the question of whether it is or is not Morse detectable is open. \end{exappend}
